column7: message

I did quote the actual article.

Maybe you should have read it before you reworded what they wrote, to make it look like 4 mil people came up just for the wedding?

At least take the time to quote the actual article:

>The tourist authority VisitBritain predicts the wedding, a worldwide TV event, will trigger a tourism boom that will last several years, eventually pulling in an extra 4m visitors and some £2bn for the country's coffers.

First off, you did nothing to prove that their "prediction" was wrong, so I guess we can assume it was accurate.  That being the case, please explain to me how an extra ~$4,000,000,000 in additional revenue means " there's absolutely no base for this claim"?

@potential-complement, @orthogonal-column, you're both missing the point. The article doesn't say 4 million people are coming JUST for the wedding, it says the wedding will trigger a tourism boom that will last several years. That's a pretty big difference. And @orthogonal-column, just because VisitBritain predicts something, doesn't mean it's going to happen. They're just trying to get people excited and boost tourism. Let's not take their predictions as fact just yet.

"@lazy-mud, thanks for clarifying the article's statement. @orthogonal-column and @potential-complement, let's focus on discussing the article's content without making assumptions or personal remarks. Remember to respect each other's opinions and avoid misrepresenting others' statements."

column7:
See I don't get this whole moral obligation to continue our species. If we don't continue our species because we find it more beneficial to us to live our lives comfortably or find a way to live immortally, then how are we hurting future generations if they don't exist?

It's the same thing as worrying about ruining the planet. The only people who consciously care about the planet are us and we only care because of how it benefits us... so why people prioritize the planets well being above humans comfort and wants is beyond me

This is EXACTLY what I wanted to ask... why is it so important that we "continue the race"? No one's going to care if no one exists to care. This is making my brain into a pretzel. 

However: we need to prioritize the planet for our sake. We can't keep drilling and mining and building ad infinitum. And we can't synthesize everything we need. We need clean air and clean water. We need the protection from natural disasters that undisturbed areas of land will give us. We need good soil for growing crops, which means we can't abuse it. Environmentalism: not just for hippies. 

Edited* to reply to second point

@frayed-cycle, @lazy-mud, you're both missing the point. It's not just about our own comfort or the planet's well-being, it's about the fact that we're a part of a larger ecosystem. Newsflash: the planet doesn't care about us, but we care about ourselves, and that's why we need to prioritize its health. It's not about "continuing the race" for the sake of some abstract moral obligation, it's about not being stupid and destroying the only home we have.

"@fixed-circuit, @frayed-cycle, and @lazy-mud, let's keep the discussion respectful and focused on the topic. We're exploring different perspectives on environmentalism and the importance of preserving our planet. @fixed-circuit, your point about being part of a larger ecosystem is a valuable one. Let's continue the conversation without personal judgments, and work towards a deeper understanding of each other's views."

column7:
Depends if the person speaking is being dismissive themselves. If they're dismissive of the wage gender gap it implies they are also dismissive of sexism in general, and are therefore more likely to be dismissed themselves (and probably rightfully so) as a man who doesn't give a crap about people who aren't men.

I was a white male vocational school director and they want BADLY to recruit girls and minorities. I questioned the directive to some extent and no one called me racist or sexist because I EARNED the right to state my opinion by (a.) my tone, (b.) my words, and (c.) my **actions**, demonstrating I care about girls and care about minority children and women's rights/civil rights... So that when I question it they are reasonably sure I'm not using dog whistles. 

Have you earned the right to argue with literal social justice activists, or do you actually believe your opinion on these causes is equal to people who dedicate their lives to these causes simply because you possess a mouth and vocal cords?

I don't want to sound like I'm questioning your sincerity, but I worry about how literal we're meant to take your examples.  Are you using hyperbole, or do you REALLY THINK that lots of activists go around believing "You cannot marry white people if you're black" or "You can't disagree with PoC because you're white," or anything else you mention.

Because those are obviously asinine ways to think, so you have to consider two possibilities: A large group of people are going around thinking asinine things and have no idea, or you're misunderstanding what's really being argued.  How open are you to the latter possibility?

@fixed-circuit, you think you're so clever don't you? Questioning my examples and implying I'm exaggerating. Let me tell you, I've seen some wild stuff in my time, and I'm not just talking about some fringe crazies on the internet. I'm talking about real life, everyday people who genuinely believe this nonsense. And as for @all-inline's comment about earning the right to argue with social justice activists, please, spare me the sanctimony. Just because someone dedicates their life to a cause doesn't mean their opinion is automatically more valuable than mine.

"@lazy-mud, let's keep the discussion civil. Refrain from personal attacks like 'you think you're so clever.' Let's focus on the topic at hand. @fixed-circuit raised a valid point about understanding the context and potential for misunderstanding. Can we discuss the issue without making assumptions about each other's intentions or intelligence?"

column7:
>So why didn't the US invade Iraq in the previous years, when Iraq was using chemical weapons against Iran? We had plenty of time beforehand to invade. So the fact remains that there were no WMDs present at the time that we did invade.

I wish they had, but better late than never

>The quote you provided does say that, and furthermore you agree with the quote.

No I dont. Al-qaida is never mentioned. But they did harbour islamist terror groups

>Do you have any evidence for this? That Saddam was any worse than any other the other dictators? And even if Saddam was the worst dictator, why are we not invading other countries with brutal dictators, now that Saddam has been dealt with?

Can you name a country committing genocide?

>  "To remove a regime that developed and used weapons of mass destruction,

They didn't have any weapons of mass destruction.

>  that harbored and supported terrorists, 

The terrorists that caused 9/11 were from Saudia Arabia.

>  committed outrageous human rights abuses, 

If presence of a brutal dictator is a criteria, there are any number of other countries controlled by brutal dictators that we can be invading.  We are not invading any of those countries.  Furthermore, the US has overthrown democratic governments and installed brutal dictators.

>  and defied the just demands of the United Nations and the world."

You mean the weapons inspectors?  Saddam let the weapons inspectors in, and they didn't find any weapons.  The weapons inspectors then tried to speak out about not finding any weapons, in an effort to prevent the war.  Their findings were suppressed, and Bush simply stated that weapons were found.  Phil Donnahue was fired because on his talk show, he tried to get the word out about not finding any weapons.

@fixed-circuit, you think you're so smart with your "facts" and "evidence", but let me tell you, you're just drinking the liberal Kool-Aid. Saddam was a bad guy, and we did the world a favor by taking him out. And by the way, what's with the obsession with WMDs? It's not like we didn't have other reasons to invade. How about the whole "spreading democracy" thing? You libs always talk about how we should be helping other countries, but when we actually do it, you complain about it. Make up your minds!

"@humid-opacity, let's keep the discussion civil and focused on the topic. Personal attacks and accusations of 'drinking the liberal Kool-Aid' aren't helpful. @fixed-circuit presented some points and evidence, and it's okay to disagree, but let's do so respectfully. Let's stick to the facts and avoid name-calling."
